human_input: Okay question one looks good. Question two looks good. Regarding question three, make sure that you're envisioning a starship-like regime. But go ahead and tweet also some future regimes that we may later enter, including the usage of ion drives and more exotic forms of propulsion like nuclear thermal rockets or nuclear pulse propulsion. And you would have to compare the same for the lunar case as well. I think the lunar power ceiling is relatively less interesting since in principle solar is sort of scalable beyond any limit that we would need to contend with. I don't think I'm interested in trying to answer this question. Yep, and Casey Hanmer has made some good breakdown of what kind of capability we might expect from a lunar mass driver. And then you should consider also what happens if we simply produce more mass drivers. So for example, if we're at the cycle limit of a single mass driver, we may just produce five. I don't immediately have interest in the idea of looking at catcher/receiver infrastructure, because one thing you could do in principle is just launch vehicles that have their own propulsion systems. You could look at it briefly, but I don't think it should be its own question. It could just tuck into a sub part of some other analysis. We should compare the case where the Moon uses all its propellant production to increase the mass throughput from the Moon. And we should look separately at the case where the Moon uses its propellant production to supplement mass throughput from Earth and see what that looks like.

human_input: Alright I worry we're overindexing on our previous analysis and at least on a skin, I don't see a compelling reason why the locks ceiling that you present is fundamentally binding. You say 50x or 500x current global supply, which I suppose seems like a lot prima facie, but it's not clear that the current locks industry has production rates that it does due to lack of demand versus due to some kind of limitation. So I think we need to double click on this and look into it more deeply if there's some literature that I'm not seeing as to why it should be binding. You can let me know. I'm sure you said somewhere also how much propellant each lost is conjuring us. -Let's respond to my prompt here, do more literature review and collect more data about what first principles or reasons these things would be binding versus what is just willingness to scale. It's helpful if you give me figures (e.g., 'this is 50x the current industry'), but that doesn't really answer the question because it's hard to say where demand would come from. So we really need to know, brass tacks: are there fundamental limits to your ability to scale this up?

outcome: q1 planetary ceilings v2c: Handmer/Terraform solar-abundance thesis modelled; LOX-as-electrolysis-byproduct established; world-picture vignettes 1 Mt→100 Tt; waste-heat ceiling computed. Codex caught CRITICAL unit confusion: I'd been using 'Tt/yr' to mean 10⁹ t/yr (Gt/yr); real teratonnes = 10¹² t/yr. Corrected: Earth chemical ceiling ~10-100 Gt/yr LEO (solar PV at 50% land tile); true cosmic Tt/yr requires 540% of land in PV — IMPOSSIBLE on Earth. Moon's architectural necessity for cosmic Tt/yr LEO is restored.
human_input: Fossil natural-gas reserves at this rate: depleted in ~17 years -> kC Handmer is working on this with his company Terraform, where they essentially want to synthetically create hydrocarbons for this purpose. It would also be to supply methane, but I guess what you're saying here is that methane synthesis is super expensive. And I suppose the KC thesis is that solar energy will be cheap and abundant, and so what you'll want is lots of fuel for storage. Let's look into that. I definitely want the just-like absolute cannot-be-crossed constraint that is: you will overheat the Earth. That would be cool to see. And how many launches are here? It would be nice as well if you would paint a picture of what the world would look like if we were entering this kind of regime where, for instance, if we're doing atmospheric chemistry to supply these thousands and thousands of rocket launches per year. What does that look like? We have these plants surrounding launch pads that are dotting the coast. What are we talking about here? I think we could lean into changing the world even more. Like you say, at 100% of US grid needs dedicated generation by this scale. But we're thinking about, you know, what is a mature and advanced Earth post-singularity doing? And so I'm not too concerned if it's like 100% of US power consumption. I'm more concerned if it's like, ah, we've tiled the oceans and 50% of all the land area with solar panels. Then we're like, oh, okay, we're getting towards a cap here. Really, really looking for these fundamental constraints, you know, post-singularity still holds.
